sourceTitle: Take these three steps before onboarding your team to Notion

questions.question: Why is it recommended to define a clear goal before starting to use Notion in an organization?

options.text: It helps you pick the cheapest subscription plan.
options.explanation: Cost is not the primary reason for goal‑setting; the article emphasizes aligning on purpose, not pricing.

options.text: It ensures the team knows why they are adopting Notion and what success looks like.
options.explanation: Correct – a clear goal provides direction, aligns expectations, and guides what you build in Notion.

options.text: It allows you to copy existing workflows without changes.
options.explanation: The guide advises against shoehorning rigid processes; goal‑setting encourages thoughtful design.

options.text: It guarantees that every employee will use Notion daily.
options.explanation: Goal‑setting doesn’t force daily usage; adoption depends on relevance and training.

questions.question: Which statement best describes Notion’s role in a company’s tool ecosystem according to the guide?

options.text: A peripheral app used only for personal note‑taking.
options.explanation: Notion is presented as a central hub, not a peripheral personal tool.

options.text: A central source of truth that can connect and embed information from other tools.
options.explanation: Correct – the guide highlights Notion as a hub that consolidates knowledge and links to other tools.

options.text: A replacement for all other software in the stack.
options.explanation: The guide suggests integration, not outright replacement of existing tools.

options.text: An optional chat platform for quick messages.
options.explanation: Notion is not described as a chat platform; it’s a workspace for structured content.

questions.question: When teaching new users the basics of Notion, which concept should be emphasized first?

options.text: How to create and move blocks.
options.explanation: Correct – the guide says Notion is built on blocks and they can be moved via drag‑and‑drop.

options.text: How to write complex SQL queries in Notion.
options.explanation: Notion does not use SQL; the focus is on blocks and simple functionality.

options.text: How to integrate third‑party APIs directly into Notion pages.
options.explanation: While possible, API integration is advanced; basics start with blocks.

options.text: How to set up a custom domain for Notion workspaces.
options.explanation: Domain setup is not a basic skill for most users; it’s outside the introductory scope.

questions.question: What is a potential risk of forcing a team to “shoehorn” its existing processes into Notion without adaptation?

options.text: Information may become fragmented across multiple Notion pages.
options.explanation: Fragmentation can happen, but the guide specifically mentions loss of information, wasted time, and incorrect work.

options.text: The team may experience lost information, wasted time, and repetitive work.
options.explanation: Correct – the article lists these exact consequences of forcing rigid processes into a flexible tool.

options.text: Notion will automatically correct the mismatched processes.
options.explanation: Notion does not auto‑correct workflows; it relies on thoughtful design.

options.text: Team members will become more satisfied with the tool instantly.
options.explanation: Forcing processes usually reduces satisfaction, not increases it.

questions.question: Which resource is NOT mentioned as a way to help teams learn Notion in the guide?

options.text: Recurring webinars on Getting Started and Basic Project Management.
options.explanation: Webinars are listed as a resource.

options.text: The Notion 101 video series for self‑paced learning.
options.explanation: The Notion 101 series is mentioned.

options.text: One‑on‑one mentorship with a Notion Certified Partner.
options.explanation: The guide does not mention a Certified Partner mentorship; it mentions CSMs for Enterprise customers.

options.text: Access to Customer Success Managers for Enterprise customers.
options.explanation: CSMs are explicitly referenced.

questions.question: How does the guide suggest you evaluate where Notion fits within your existing tech stack?

options.text: By counting how many tools your company currently uses and aiming to replace half of them with Notion.
options.explanation: The guide advises understanding the ecosystem, not replacing tools arbitrarily.

options.text: By identifying Notion as a central hub that can embed content and act as connective tissue between other tools.
options.explanation: Correct – the article describes Notion as a hub and connective tissue via embeds and API.

options.text: By ensuring every team member logs into Notion before any other application.
options.explanation: Login order isn’t discussed as a factor for fit.

options.text: By migrating all existing data into Notion first, then deciding on other tools later.
options.explanation: The guide emphasizes integration, not a wholesale data migration before evaluating fit.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of the ‘template gallery’ mentioned at the end of the guide?

options.text: To provide pre‑built structures that teams can adapt for their own workflows.
options.explanation: Correct – the gallery offers over 10,000 templates to help teams start quickly.

options.text: To sell premium Notion subscriptions directly to users.
options.explanation: The gallery is a resource, not a sales portal.

options.text: To host community forums for Notion users.
options.explanation: Forums are not mentioned; the focus is on templates.

options.text: To list all third‑party integrations available for Notion.
options.explanation: Integrations are discussed elsewhere; the gallery is for templates.

questions.question: According to the guide, why is it important to remind teammates that there’s ‘no correct way to do things’ in Notion?

options.text: Because Notion enforces a single workflow for every team, and deviating causes errors.
options.explanation: Notion does not enforce a single workflow; flexibility is a core feature.

options.text: Because encouraging flexibility helps teams explore Notion’s capabilities and find the best fit for their needs.
options.explanation: Correct – the guide highlights Notion’s flexibility and the value of experimentation.

options.text: Because it reduces the need for any training or onboarding materials.
options.explanation: Training is still valuable; the point is to foster a mindset of exploration.

options.text: Because it ensures all teams will use identical page structures across the organization.
options.explanation: The opposite is true; Notion supports diverse structures.
